title: Lemon posset tart
subtitle: Sharp lemon posset set in a crisp shortcrust shell
excerpt: Lemon posset sets by acid alone: cream, sugar, and lemon juice cooked briefly on the hob until the citric acid causes the cream to thicken as it chills. No eggs, no gelatine. Poured into a blind-baked pastry shell and refrigerated until firm. It slices cleanly and tastes aggressively of lemon.
sourceNotes: Posset was originally a warm medieval British drink of hot milk curdled with wine or ale. The modern dessert form, in which cream is set by lemon juice without eggs, became popular in British home cooking in the late twentieth century. The tart format, where the posset is set in a pastry shell rather than individual glasses, is a more recent home-baking adaptation. The recipe follows the standard British posset method.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The tart shell can be blind-baked a day ahead. The assembled tart keeps in the fridge for up to 2 days. The posset continues to firm with time, which is not a problem.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven for blind baking the pastry shell only; the posset filling is not baked

glossaryTerms.definition: Lemon posset sets because citric acid from the lemon juice denatures the proteins in the cream, causing it to thicken as it chills. No additional setting agent is needed. The mixture must be boiled for the full 3 minutes to reduce sufficiently, and the lemon juice must be added after removing from the heat and then reheated briefly. If the posset does not set after 3 hours in the fridge, it was not reduced enough. The ratio of cream to juice is critical: too little juice and it will not set; too much and the texture becomes grainy.

content.text: Lemon posset is the simplest dessert filling with no eggs, no gelatine, and no bain-marie. Understanding the
means you will not be tempted to add stabilisers it does not need. Measure the lemon juice accurately and give the mixture the full boil time.

Rub 100 g cold butter into 200 g flour and 30 g icing sugar. Add 1 egg yolk and 1 to 2 tbsp cold water to a firm pastry. Wrap and refrigerate for 30 minutes. Heat the oven to 190 degrees C fan. Roll to 3 mm and line a greased 23 cm tart tin. Chill for 15 minutes. Line with baking paper and baking weights. Blind-bake for 15 minutes. Remove paper and weights and bake for a further 10 to 12 minutes until the base is golden and dry. Cool completely.

Bring 600 ml double cream, 150 g caster sugar, and the zest of 3 lemons to a full boil in a saucepan. Boil, stirring, for 3 minutes. Remove from the heat. Add the juice of 3 lemons (about 100 to 110 ml) and stir well. Return to a very brief boil for 30 seconds. Leave to cool for 10 minutes, stirring occasionally, then pour through a sieve into the cooled pastry shell. Refrigerate for at least 2 hours until set. Decorate with a little lemon zest if you like.

items.problem: Posset has not set after 3 hours
items.solution: The mixture was not boiled long enough, or the juice was measured inaccurately. You need at least 100 ml of fresh lemon juice. Bottled juice has lower acidity and may not set reliably.

items.problem: Filling shrinks away from the pastry sides after chilling
items.solution: The posset contracted as it set. This is normal and does not affect the flavour. Pour the posset into the shell while it is still warm; letting it cool fully before pouring increases the chance of shrinkage.
